metadata.title: The Seller With the Best Real Track Record
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = Laplace rule / sample size correction / reliable estimate; CRIMSON = raw percentage / small sample / perfect-score trap
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Bayesian updating over full curve, no beta distribution, no confidence intervals. Wonder register.

signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Three sellers: 100 percent from 10 reviews, 96 percent from 50 reviews, 93 percent from 200 reviews. Most people pick the 100 percent seller. That is almost certainly the wrong choice.

card.copy: The Seller With the Best Real Track Record

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Three sellers: 100 percent from 10 reviews, 96 percent from 50 reviews, 93 percent from 200 reviews. Most people pick the 100 percent seller. That is almost cer

beats.narration_text: A seller at 95 percent true success rate will produce a perfect 10 out of 10 run about 60 percent of the time. The perfect score is common for a mediocre seller. It tells you much less than it seems.

production_viz.label: P(10/10 | p=0.95) = 0.95^10 ≈ 60% calculation; CRIMSON for misleading perfect score; wide likelihood curve at 10 reviews

beats.narration_text: With 10, 50, and 200 reviews, how should you weight the raw percentages? What formula converts observed ratings into reliable probability estimates?

card.copy: 100%/10 reviews vs 96%/50 reviews vs 93%/200 reviews. How do you correctly weight sample size against raw percentage?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: With 10, 50, and 200 reviews, how should you weight the raw percentages? What formula converts observed ratings into reliable probability estimates?

beats.narration_text: The binomial likelihood: the probability of the observed track record if the seller's true rate is p. With 10 reviews, the likelihood curve is wide and flat. With 200 reviews, the curve is narrow — the data tightly constrains p.

production_viz.label: Three binomial likelihood curves: Seller A wide/flat (10 reviews), Seller B medium (50 reviews), Seller C narrow (200 reviews); TEAL for trustworthy narrow curve

beats.narration_text: Laplace's rule of succession: add one pseudo-success and one pseudo-failure to every seller's record. Seller A: 10 out of 10 → 11 out of 12 equals 91.7 percent. Seller B: 48 out of 50 → 49 out of 52 equals 94.2 percent. Seller C: 186 out of 200 → 187 out of 202 equals 92.6 percent.

production_viz.label: Laplace +1/+1 applied: A 11/12=91.7%, B 49/52=94.2%, C 187/202=92.6%; TEAL for Laplace-corrected values; CRIMSON for misleading raw values

beats.narration_text: Best bet: Seller B at 94.2 percent — not the 100 percent seller at 91.7 percent. Laplace's rule shrinks extreme estimates toward 50 percent, with more shrinkage at small sample sizes.

production_viz.label: Final comparison: A=91.7%, B=94.2% (winner circled in TEAL), C=92.6%; shrinkage toward 50% shown with more shrinkage for A (small n)

beats.narration_text: Why add one pseudo-success and one pseudo-failure? In the Bayesian framework this corresponds to a uniform prior — all true rates equally plausible before seeing any data. The Laplace estimate is the posterior mean.

production_viz.label: Uniform prior over [0,1] + data = posterior; Laplace estimate = posterior mean labeled in TEAL; Bayesian justification

beats.narration_text: Probability of a perfect 10 out of 10 at 95 percent true rate: 0.95 to the 10th equals about 60 percent. So a 95-percent seller produces a perfect score more than half the time. The perfect score carries very little information.

production_viz.label: 0.95^10 ≈ 60% calculation with bar; perfect score common even for mediocre seller; CRIMSON for misleading perfect score informativeness

beats.narration_text: With large samples, Laplace barely changes the answer. Seller C: 186 over 200 versus 187 over 202 — tiny difference. The correction matters most when n is small — exactly when raw percentages are most misleading.

production_viz.label: Correction size vs sample size: large correction at n=10 (CRIMSON), tiny at n=200 (TEAL); Laplace adjustment shrinks with n

beats.narration_text: Laplace rule: add 1 success and 1 failure to each track record. Seller A: 91.7 percent. Seller B: 94.2 percent — winner. Seller C: 92.6 percent. Small n means more shrinkage. Perfect score from 10 reviews happens 60 percent of the time at 95 percent true rate.

card.copy: Laplace +1/+1: A=91.7%, B=94.2% (winner), C=92.6%. Perfect 10/10 at 95% true rate: happens 60% of the time. Sample size matters more than raw percentage.
card.sub: Laplace's rule: the right way to rank sellers

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Laplace rule: add 1 success and 1 failure to each track record. Seller A: 91.7 percent. Seller B: 94.2 percent — winner. Seller C: 92.6 percent. Small n means m

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
